previous_statement: CONSERVATION LAW: For free particles, partial-beta T-alpha-beta = 0 distributionally. Proof uses distributional derivative, chain rule on test functions, integration by parts. Boundary terms vanish by compact support of phi and properness of worldline. Verify each step and the treatment of boundary terms.
new_statement: CONSERVATION LAW: For free particles, partial-beta T-alpha-beta = 0 distributionally. Proof uses: (i) standard distributional derivative definition (integration by parts with sign), (ii) chain rule on test functions, (iii) smoothness of p^alpha = m gamma-dot^alpha from smooth worldline definition, (iv) integration by parts justified by properness of gamma (Proposition well-definedness) giving compact support of phi(gamma(tau)) in R. Boundary terms vanish; for free particles the right-hand side of the IBP formula vanishes. The force density case retains the nonzero dp^alpha/dtau integral. The remark uses the correct multivariable chain rule (d/dt)delta^{(3)}(x-gamma(t)) = -(d gamma^i/dt)(partial/partial x^i)delta^{(3)}(x-gamma(t)).
